stories.headline: Bitcoin steadies near $61-62K over the weekend after week-long slide; RSI flags oversold
stories.summary: Bitcoin stabilized over the weekend, trading around $61,500-$62,500 on Sunday after a punishing week that broke below $62,000 Friday. The 14-day RSI fell near 24 — deeply oversold — prompting talk of a relief bounce, with $63,000 the key overhead level. The slide was driven by ETF outflows, profit-taking and a hawkish Fed repricing after the strong May jobs report; ether traded near $1,700.

perspectives.keyFraming: After a sharp washout, oversold conditions set up a possible bounce; long-term holders see the dip as opportunity.
perspectives.summary: Technical analysts note bitcoin held above its weekend low with RSI in deeply oversold territory, a classic setup for a short-term rebound, with $63,000 the line that decides the next move. For bulls, the macro-driven flush rather than any structural failure represents a buying opportunity once Fed and Middle East uncertainty clears.

sources.title: Bitcoin Holds Above $59.1K Low as Short-Term Charts Signal Oversold Bounce Setup

stories.headline: Nasdaq sinks ~4% in worst day since April 2025 as Broadcom's soft AI outlook triggers a chip-stock rout
stories.summary: The Nasdaq fell about 4% to 25,709, the S&P 500 dropped 2.6% and the Dow lost roughly 695 points as a semiconductor selloff wiped out hundreds of billions in market value. The slide was triggered by Broadcom, which reported 143% AI-revenue growth but guided next-quarter AI revenue below expectations, sinking its stock about 13% and dragging Nvidia, AMD, Micron and Arm lower. Hot jobs data and rising Treasury yields amplified the move.

perspectives.keyFraming: Framed as Broadcom's strong-but-not-blowout AI outlook puncturing lofty expectations and detonating a broad semiconductor selloff.
perspectives.summary: Market coverage detailed the Nasdaq's roughly 4% drop — its worst day since April 2025 — and tied it to Broadcom's guidance, with chipmakers Nvidia, AMD, Micron and Arm falling and a hot jobs report and higher yields adding pressure.

sources.title: Stock Market Today (June 5, 2026): Nasdaq falls 4% as semiconductor slide wipes $1T from markets
Nasdaq falls 4% and suffers worst day since April 2025 as traders flee chip stocks

perspectives.keyFraming: Framed as a healthy reality check for a priced-for-perfection chip sector, not a break in the AI thesis.
perspectives.summary: Market commentary noted that even 143% AI revenue growth was not enough for a sector priced for perfection, casting the selloff as a long-overdue correction in overheated semiconductor valuations rather than a thesis-breaking event.

sources.title: Chip selloff erases over $1 trillion in stock market value

stories.headline: Oil holds in the mid-$90s, up about 4% on the week, as stalled Iran talks and Gulf strikes keep a risk premium
stories.summary: Brent crude traded around the mid-$90s a barrel on June 5, up more than 4% on the week, with WTI near the low $90s. Prices stayed elevated on stalled US-Iran negotiations, renewed Gulf military activity and Iranian pressure on the Strait of Hormuz, even as US pump prices stayed sharply higher with crude still below $100.

perspectives.keyFraming: Markets coverage ties the elevated price to a geopolitical risk premium and tighter OPEC spare capacity rather than demand strength.
perspectives.summary: Energy reporting noted Brent holding in the mid-$90s and up roughly 4% on the week, attributing the strength to stalled US-Iran diplomacy, Gulf military activity and reduced OPEC spare capacity, while flagging that intraday benchmark levels varied by data source.

sources.title: Brent Crude Oil - Price

perspectives.keyFraming: Coverage frames the war-risk premium and elevated pump prices as a case for boosting US output and export capacity.
perspectives.summary: Market commentary highlighted that US gasoline averages climbed sharply as crude held a war-risk premium below $100, presenting the squeeze on consumers against the backdrop of Middle East supply risk and a stronger weekly oil tape.

sources.title: Current price of oil as of June 5, 2026

stories.headline: Wall Street suffers worst day of the year Friday as chip selloff and Fed hike fears erase ~$1T
stories.summary: US stocks closed sharply lower Friday, June 5: the Nasdaq fell 4.2% to 25,709 — its worst session since April 2025 — as a semiconductor rout wiped roughly $1 trillion from the market. The S&P 500 dropped 2.6% and the Dow lost 1.3%. The slide followed a hotter-than-expected May jobs report (172,000) that lifted rate-hike odds. Markets are closed over the weekend; the FOMC meets June 16-17, its first under Chair Kevin Warsh.

perspectives.keyFraming: A strong labor market collided with a hawkish Fed tilt, exposing stretched AI valuations in a rare everything-selloff.
perspectives.summary: The Nasdaq's 4.2% plunge marked the worst day of 2026 as traders fled chip stocks and braced for a possible rate hike after 172,000 jobs were added in May. A simultaneous selloff in equities, Treasuries, bitcoin and gold signaled broad de-risking rather than a simple rotation, with elevated AI valuations the vulnerable point. Attention turns to the June 16-17 FOMC, the first chaired by Kevin Warsh.

sources.title: Nasdaq, S&P 500 suffer worst day of year as AI stocks tumble and Fed rate-hike odds rise
Nasdaq falls 4% and suffers worst day since April 2025 as traders flee chip stocks

perspectives.keyFraming: A robust jobs print is good news, but it forces the Fed's hand and punctures speculative excess in AI and crypto.
perspectives.summary: Friday's selloff was driven by a labor market that refuses to cool — 172,000 jobs versus forecasts near half that — reviving the prospect that Warsh's Fed has to tighten. Coverage framed the semiconductor slide as a roughly $1 trillion repricing of richly valued AI names, with the broad decline across asset classes underscoring how much froth had built up.

sources.title: Nasdaq falls 4% as semiconductor slide wipes $1T from markets
How major US stock indexes fared Friday 6/5/2026

stories.headline: Oil turns higher as Iran-Israel war reignites; OPEC+ approves fourth symbolic output hike since Hormuz closure
stories.summary: Crude reversed its weekly slide as renewed Iran-Israel strikes on June 7-8 revived the war premium and dimmed hopes for a deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, shut since late February. Brent ended the week up more than 4% near $94 and WTI near $91. On June 7 OPEC+ approved a fourth output-quota hike (+188,000 bpd for July), though the blockade makes most of the added barrels undeliverable. Weak Chinese demand had capped earlier gains.

perspectives.keyFraming: A brief diplomatic thaw pulled the war premium out of crude, but renewed strikes snapped prices back up and the Hormuz blockade keeps a floor under them.
perspectives.summary: Oil had retreated earlier in the week on diplomatic signals, but the June 7-8 missile exchange between Iran and Israel sent crude back up, with Brent finishing the week more than 4% higher. Analysts say the Hormuz blockade keeps a floor under prices and that sustained relief depends on traffic through the strait actually resuming.

sources.title: Opec agrees fourth monthly output rise despite Hormuz closure and price swings

perspectives.keyFraming: OPEC+ supply discipline and the Hormuz chokepoint mean energy prices stay elevated, and the new quota hike is symbolic while the strait stays shut.
perspectives.summary: The structural picture stays tight: the strait has been effectively closed since late February, so OPEC+'s fourth consecutive quota increase is largely cosmetic — delegates concede most of the added barrels cannot physically reach customers. Until shipping normalizes, US consumers face elevated pump prices regardless of diplomatic headlines.

sources.title: OPEC+ approves fourth oil output quota hike since Strait of Hormuz closure

stories.headline: Brent jumps above $97 as renewed Iran-Israel strikes revive the war premium
stories.summary: Oil surged Monday June 8 after Iran and Israel traded direct strikes over the weekend, reversing a two-session slide driven by weak Chinese demand. Brent rose about 4.9% toward $97.7 a barrel and WTI about 3.4% toward $93.6, with an Iranian missile reportedly hitting a Mahshahr petrochemical plant. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed, and OPEC+'s fourth consecutive quota hike (+188,000 bpd for July) does little while Gulf barrels can't physically reach customers.

perspectives.keyFraming: A geopolitical shock reverses a demand-driven slide, as a fresh war premium is priced back into crude.
perspectives.summary: Brent and WTI rebounded sharply after Sunday's missile exchange between Iran and Israel — Tehran's first barrage since the early-April ceasefire — threatening the diplomatic track. The jump unwound Friday's drop to about $93 on weak Chinese imports. Analysts note OPEC+ raised paper quotas it cannot fill while Hormuz stays shut, leaving supply tight.

sources.title: Fragile ceasefire in jeopardy as Iran reportedly fires first missiles at Israel
OPEC+ approves fourth oil output quota hike since Strait of Hormuz closure

perspectives.keyFraming: Trump leverages ceasefire diplomacy to cap the spike, signaling sanctions relief is possible only if Iran 'behaves.'
perspectives.summary: Right-leaning coverage emphasized Trump's intervention — demanding both sides 'immediately stop shooting' and floating conditional sanctions easing — as the lever capping the oil spike, casting the president as managing both the war and its economic fallout for US consumers facing elevated pump prices.

sources.title: Trump says US open to unfreezing Iranian funds, easing sanctions if they behave

stories.headline: US stocks rebound as chip names rally and Iran signals de-escalation; small caps lag sharply
stories.summary: US equities recovered Monday after last week's AI-led rout, as Iran said it had ended its operation against Israel and Trump signaled ceasefire progress. The S&P 500 rose about 0.9%, the Nasdaq about 1.4% and the Dow about 0.6%, led by semiconductors — Intel jumped roughly 10% on a reported Google chip order. But the small-cap Russell 2000 fell about 3.5% as the 10-year Treasury yield hit a two-week high near 4.57% on Fed-hike fears.

perspectives.keyFraming: The relief rally is narrow and fragile — megacap AI and chip names mask broad weakness in rate-sensitive small caps.
perspectives.summary: A bounce in chipmakers lifted headline indexes off last week's lows, but the internals were poor: the Russell 2000 dropped about 3.5% as the 10-year yield neared 4.57% on rate-hike fears. The rally hinged on Iran's claim it had halted operations and Trump's optimism about a deal, with the Lebanon front and Hormuz blockade still unresolved.

sources.title: Stock Market Today (June 8, 2026): Nasdaq, S&P 500 rebound after weekend Middle East exchange

perspectives.keyFraming: Markets are voting for the AI capex supercycle and de-escalation; a chip-led rebound shows resilience despite Fed-hike noise.
perspectives.summary: Investors rotated back into AI winners as Middle East tensions cooled. Intel surged about 10% on a reported three-million-unit Google chip order, Marvell jumped on news it will join the S&P 500, and Corning rose on an Amazon data-center fiber deal — concrete signs the AI build-out keeps driving orders. Bond yields rose on a strong labor market that bulls read as economic strength.

sources.title: Marvell Technology jumps after news it will join the S&P 500 index

stories.headline: 10-year Treasury yield climbs to a two-week high as traders price about 70% odds of a December Fed hike
stories.summary: The US 10-year Treasury yield rose to about 4.57% Monday, its highest in two weeks, extending a selloff that began after May's 172k jobs print with unemployment steady at 4.3%. Swaps now imply a quarter-point Fed hike by December, with market-implied odds near 70%. The Fed's target range stands at 3.50%-3.75% under new Chair Kevin Warsh, with markets assigning over 95% odds of no change at the June 16-17 meeting.

perspectives.keyFraming: A strong labor market gives the Fed cover to stay disciplined on inflation; markets pricing a hike is normalization, not panic.
perspectives.summary: May's 172k jobs and 4.3% unemployment show an economy that can absorb tighter policy. With Warsh signaling resolve, a December hike would reinforce credibility against energy-shock inflation, while over 95% odds of a June hold show no crisis — just a return to data dependence.

sources.title: See Odds for Next Fed Rate Hike

stories.headline: Bitcoin rebounds about 4% after dipping below $60,000; ether jumps about 8% on Middle East relief
stories.summary: Crypto bounced Monday after last week's slide. Bitcoin traded around $63,300, up about 4% on the day, after briefly falling below $60,000 over the weekend; ether rose about 8% near $1,690. The rebound tracked broader risk-on sentiment as Iran signaled de-escalation. Analysts blamed the prior decline on war-driven risk-off flows, capital rotating into AI equities, ETF outflows and selling by large holders.

perspectives.keyFraming: Crypto remains a high-beta risk asset, not a haven — it sold off into the war and is bouncing only because stocks did.
perspectives.summary: Bitcoin's drop below $60k and snap-back show it trading like leveraged tech rather than 'digital gold.' Money has flowed to AI names instead, and large-holder selling plus ETF outflows leave the rebound on shaky footing as geopolitical risk lingers.

sources.title: Bitcoin and ethereum prices today, Monday, June 8, 2026

perspectives.keyFraming: A fast recovery off sub-$60k shows underlying resilience; once geopolitical fear fades, crypto reclaims ground alongside risk assets.
perspectives.summary: Bitcoin's roughly 4% bounce and ether's 8% pop the moment tensions cooled signal buyers ready to step in on dips. Backers argue the selloff was macro-driven rather than structural, and that the asset class tends to lead recoveries when risk appetite returns.

sources.title: Current price of Bitcoin for June 8, 2026
